moment_text: You know, my inflection point came early, but it didn't happen in one step. In my early twenties when I was still at college, I was diagnosed with cervical cancer, which was normally a simple fix, but unfortunately had the laser therapy and the radiation, but it came back and it had mutated. And at that point, the therapies that they gave me. Were really life changing and not things that I wanted to do. Uh, basically, you know, full hysterectomy, et cetera. And I found a different way and found a holistic doctor, which remember this was 40 years ago, you know, when I first heard a holistic doctor, what the heck is that? Wow. And I was able to meld. Nutrition and herbology supplements and western medicine and went into remission, and I've been cancer free since.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong agency signals (seeking alternative care) in response to a challenging medical situation, interacting with the healthcare system.

moment_text: You would think that would absolutely do it for me, but I was in my early twenties, felt invincible, so still didn't fully listen to myself. I did in many ways, researched and whenever there was something going on for myself emotionally or physically. I wouldn't just take the first answer for the thing. I would really kind of research, and then when I finally deemed myself ready to have a child, which is something I had always wanted to do, but I had put off because of career, because of childhood stuff, et cetera, I dealt with infertility. And even though on the inside I was saying what these fertility doctors is not it. It took me five years of eight miscarriages, a lot of money spent on iis and IVFs. To finally listen to myself. I found a fertility doctor that found something serious but simple to fix and had my daughter, and that was really, it was like the inflection point started in my twenties, and that's where it concretized.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains clear barrier signals (delayed diagnosis, cost) and agency signals (self_advocacy, changing providers) within a healthcare system interaction.

moment_text: From then on, I realized, listen, listened to the inner voice, to the instinct, and that took me through a bad bout of Lyme disease and then. When I went into perimenopause and menopause, which was kind of intense, uh, because I had been going through the lys, et cetera, I realized I listened and knew that what I was getting in terms of therapies and uh, suggestions from the medical community, and this was about nine years ago, so there was a lot less was not it. And I was complaining to my husband. I was looking, and between all of that and my husband's great storytelling mind, we ended up making a film that talks about the fact that menopause is actually an evolutionary adaptation. And that postmenopausal females are what helped our species become the hu, the humans, the homo sapiens that we are.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment describes a pattern of agency (listening to inner voice) in response to a recurring barrier (lack of information from the medical community) across multiple life stages.

moment_text: I didn't see myself that way at that time. And I really did wanna become a mom at some point in my life. I guess that's when I like, no, and that's where Providence or the universe, God. Whatever you're connected to came into being. I was working for the university call center and our university had a medical school. And that night when I had gotten a third doctor saying the same thing, they put me on calling medical alumni. I was just doing my job. And when I called one of the doctors, he said, no, uh, take me off the list and you're supposed to get him talking. And that's when he said. The university taught me how to treat humans like cars, and I can't support that. And so I, I thought, well, are you still a doctor? Yes, I'm a holistic physician now. And that's the first time I had heard that. And that's where I said, can I talk to you about what's going on? And he said, yeah, come to my clinic, see my partner. She's actually more apt to help you. And that's how I found it. So. I guess what I would say is I just kept going instead of shutting down and just going back to my dorm room and like curling up. I just kept going and when something came up I trusted because I could have kept going for rote and just said, yeah, thank you very much, and gone to the next call. But I realized I was being given something and I took advantage of it.

moment_text: Now in my post-menopausal years, I feel much better when I eat much more plant-based, but I'm a lifelong omnivore and there's meat dishes that are my comfort foods.
